utterances: Hello.
So, how do you use static from television in art?
Well, it's a wonderful pattern to take pictures of.
It's random and it's constantly changing.
And if you haven't looked at static real closely, you may not realize there's a lot of different kinds.
You can have large dots and small dots.
And I like to think of them like digital, you know, waves breaking.
And if you look at them closely, they're kind of mesmerizing.
So, you actually take photographs of static from a television.
Well, I not only take photographs, I will actually adjust the static and play with it with different channels and different settings.
And in some you add color.
Well, first of all, there's color built into a television, for example.
So, a lot of the static dots that you're looking at will be tinged with color, and I can enhance that when I process them.
So, they sort of come out like abstract paintings, like almost like a digital Jackson Pollock.
Absolutely.
Well said, yeah.
I love the Abstract Expressionist painting of the '50s, like Pollock.
And I was amazed that some of them, you know, had that feel to them, like a de Kooning or something like that.
Why static, Mr. Doble?
What was it that sort of moved you to work with television static?
When I was 12 years old, we got our first television.
I lived on a mountaintop in Connecticut.
And we were about 100 miles from New York, and we could get very, very fuzzy signals.
And this is during the golden age of television.
So, I would play hooky and…
(Soundbite of laughter)
…and I would tune the television.

I would turn the rabbit ears back and forth, and lengthen them until I could get the clearest fuzzy signal, which is very satisfying to take a marginal signal and turn it into a clear one, and knowing that I wasn't in school, either, it was wonderful.
You have an explanation on your Web site about the relationship between static and the Big Bang Theory.
What's that all about?
Well, according to everything I've read, a small percentage of the static that you see on an analog television, when it's tuned to an empty channel, is from the Big Bang.
It's really the echo of the Big Bang.
It's called cosmic microwave background radiation or CMBR.
And this is being picked up by the television signal.
Right...
What they're going to do is weigh the constitutionality of the so-called partial birth abortion ban.
This is a piece of federal law from 2003, and the law is controversial because it does not contain an exception allowing for this procedure, even in the event of a danger to the health of the mother.
The reason this is important is that because in 2000, the Supreme Court struck down a Nebraska partial birth abortion ban that similarly had no health exception.
O'Connor said bring us back a law with a health exception and maybe we'll find it constitutional.
Congress went ahead and passed an act that had no health exception.
The court has agreed to hear whether a substantially similar law to the one that they struck down is now going to be constitutional.
And so does that indicate that they may reverse themselves?
Well, certainly none of the lower courts have upheld this ban.
In fact, this ban has never gone into effect.
So the very fact that the court agreed to hear it suggests they're at least willing to take a position that might be different from the one in which O'Connor was the key vote in 2000.
Okay, another big issue: affirmative action.
What are the issues involved here?
Again, Madeleine, these are cases that we thought had been settled with O'Connor being the deciding vote only a few years ago.
But this involved a pair of affirmative action cases in high school context, not in the college context - one coming out of Seattle, Washington, one coming out of Jefferson City, Kentucky.
Race is used as part of the way to determine which schools these kids are being put into in high school, and there's a question about whether or not that violates the constitution.
Again, we thought this was settled in the University of Michigan cases that the court only just recently heard.
Raises the question yet again, is this about changes in the law or about changes in the composition of the court?
And I understand these cases are brought by white students or the parents of white students who object to the local laws?
That's right.
They say that using race - particularly in a fixed numeric kind of quota program - violates their children's' constitutional rights.
Okay, and finally global warming.
This is a - the first time I believe the court has heard a case involving global warming.
Tell us about this.
This is an incredibly interesting case.
We had a little bit of a foreshadow of it in the last term when there was an important environmental case that had to do with water regulations.
But this has to do with whether the environmental protectioncy(ph) agency has the authority to regulate greenhouse gasses that lead to the so-called greenhouse effect.
The EPA said since there's real scientific uncertainty about whether there is a greenhouse affect, they're not going to regulate the carbon emissions.
And twelve states have - disagree with that and have sued.
So this is a really important test case about whether or not an agency can say, well, because of scientific uncertainty we're just not going to regulate this effect.
All right, Dahlia.
Let's talk about the make up of the court.
This is the first full-term of the new Roberts court, but from what I understand, most of these cases will be decided by a justice who's been around for a while -Justice Anthony Kennedy.
That's right, Madeleine.

I just don't think you can overstate how critical his vote is going to be on a court that is essentially - on most of these key issues, affirmative action, abortion, and the environment - pretty polarized, four-four, with him in the middle.
One wag recently observed that this is Kennedy's world now.
We all just live in it.
Dahlia Lithwick is legal analyst for Slate.com and for us here at DAY TO DAY.
Thank you, Dahlia
It's Tuesday, the day we read from your emails and blog comments.
We took the show global last week with our first broadcast as Talk of the World.
We asked our listeners from many countries to tell us about the reaction they saw to President-elect Barack Obama.
Barbara Robertson sent us this dispatch from Morogoro in Tanzania.
I live and work just outside of Daresalam.
After the US election, one of the national Swahili newspaper's headlines read, it is possible with a colored photo of Mr. Obama on the front page.
The thing I look forward to was a new era of diplomacy at least here in Tanzania.
Many have seen the Bush-Cheney often as bullies, and that is very antithetical to the way people live here, attempting to live peaceably by talking to both one's friends and one's enemies.
Wouldn't it be nice if we could have a dialogue with other nations again?
Shafik(ph) Raymond tuned into the show in Pakistan and gave us one view from his part of the world.
My concern is related to the way the administration will deal with the dictatorial governments in the region.
I feel one of the reasons for growing terrorism is the way dictators suppress the public opinion.
I think there will be a policy change by the Democratic government, distress for democratically-elected governments in the region.
I'm optimistic about it because I remember when President Clinton visited Pakistan, he refused to meet General Pervez Musharraf.
I think by changing the policy and supporting democratically-elected governments, American can overcome the problem of terrorism.
If you missed the Talk Of The World special, you can always find our past programs on our website.
To download or to listen to online, that's at npr.org/talk.
Many of you wrote to take issue with Jasmyne Cannick, our guest on the Opinion Page yesterday.
She argued that the campaign against California's ban on gay marriage dropped the ball with black voters.
She is black and she is a lesbian, she explained.
But black voters do not see this issue as equal to civil rights.
Sarah emailed from Oklahoma City to disagree.
I am a lesbian who served seven years in the Air Force.
I understand your guest's views regarding the fact the civil rights issue behind gay marriage does not compare to the civil rights of the black community.
However, even though people see me as a white woman when I was in the military, I couldn't tell people my true identity.
I had to lie and live a double life, which is ultimately the reason I decided to get out of the military.
Gay rights is a civil rights issue.

Gays and lesbians have endured plenty of abuse from everyone including black people for many years and it feels like nobody fights for us except people of our own community regardless of race especially when a constitution is changed to prohibit gays and lesbians to be married.
Tony Blankley, editorial page editor of the Washington Times, speaking with us from Washington.
Tony, thank you.
Thank you.
So in the stuff that you write about on your blog and on your Instagram account you are talking about what it means to be Indonesian and all these different foods from all over.
But also, by writing in English, you're doing something a little broader even than just Indonesia.
Yes.
I thought, why not, you know, use English to introduce all our food?
And people can read about it and, you know, maybe decide to visit Indonesia somehow.
Do you ever try fish meatballs?
Yeah.
Yeah, this just kind of like that, but just drier.
No soup, just peanut sauce.
You should try one.
Yes.
Yeah, it smells leafy from the banana leaf, and also from the grill there's a little smokiness.
Yeah.
It's shaped almost like a Tootsie Roll.
Oh, yeah.
You can say that, too.
Indonesia is a country where half the population is below the age of 30.
And so I wonder how you view your future.
If you ask me about my generation and my father's generation, my dad has no luxury in finding creativity.
I mean, he just worked just to let us live.

When you say your father just worked to pay the bills, are you saying that your parents' generation would never understand spending all of your time and energy photographing food for an Instagram account?
Yes.
My dad used to be very skeptical about it.
Why are you doing this?
You are not making money.
You are not doing good things.
But as time passes by, my dad's starting to see what I do - what I try to do, actually.
And now he's starting to see the good side.
Is there one food in particular here that reminds you of your childhood?
Oh, of course.
The one - soto medan...
What is soto medan?
It's coconut milk-based, so it's kind of thick with a lot of coriander and a lot of stuff like that.
And it feels - especially right now it's raining, right?
- so it's really good for this moment.
